ralph_hertwig.title: The ABCs of Bounded Rationality: Adaptive Rules for an Uncertain World
ralph_hertwig.abstract: Herbert Simon’s notion of bounded rationality represent, in my view, a research program with three aims: (1) to study the actual processes of decision making rather than as-if models of expected-utility maximization; (2) to analyze the heuristics and strategies used under genuine uncertainty and computational intractability, not merely under risk or ambiguity; and (3) to investigate how minds adapt to their environments by explicating the underlying mechanisms, rather than modeling the mind or the environment in isolation. In this talk, I will survey the repertoire of heuristics—the adaptive toolbox—available to individuals, institutions, collectives, and policymakers. I will then trace how the study of bounded rationality has developed in psychology and economics, and how it has often been recast primarily as a catalog of deviations from classical standards of rationality, with too little attention to how strategies fit their environments. Next, I will ask why people use heuristics, focusing on accuracy–effort trade-offs and, more importantly, on the surprising robustness of simple rules under limited knowledge. Finally, I will address the important objections that boundedly rational minds and heuristics will fail in strategic interactions.
ralph_hertwig.bio: Ralph Hertwig is Director of the Center for Adaptive Rationality at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin. Hertwig’s research focuses on models of bounded rationality such as simple heuristics, on the importance of learning and decisions from experience, on the measurement of risk preferences, and on ways to change people’s behavior for the better by boosting their competences. His articles have been published in top-tier scientific journals, such as Psychological Review, Behavioral and Brain Sciences, Psychological Bulletin, Trends in Cognitive Sciences, Psychological Science, Nature Human Behaviour, and PNAS. He has co-authored and edited five books, the newest one on Boost will come on the market in January 2027\. Hertwig has received various prizes for his work, the most important one is the Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Prize, Germany’s most prestigious research award. In 2026, he received the „Allais Memorial Prize“ —  the prize is awarded annually for outstanding achievements in the field of behavioral sciences. As a member of the German National Academy of Sciences Leopoldina, Hertwig has been involved in providing independent scientific advice to the German government on a range of behavioral public policy issues.  He is a member of acatech – National Academy of Science and Engineering, and of the Berlin Brandenburg Academy of Sciences and Humanities.

konstantinos_katsikopoulos.title: Operationalizing Bounded Rationality
konstantinos_katsikopoulos.abstract: Trust your gut. Don’t rely on urges—analyze\! Decisions are either logical or psychological… Such popular maxims reveal a complicated, even confused approach to how one should make decisions. The sciences of decision theory and practice suggest that managers should leverage the power of mathematics, while also reserving a role for personal insights. But how exactly can that be achieved? Combining analysis and intuition sounds like trying to have one’s cake and eat it too; enticing but impossible. We believe that it is indeed possible to combine analysis and intuition to make decisions. And that doing so in a systematic way is now within reach. A slow yet powerful series of discoveries has culminated in a vision of decision-making that brings ever closer together mathematics, psychology and management in the form of analytical models of intuition. The concept that enables these advances is *fast-and-frugal heuristics*. The present piece (*i*) provides a background for fast-and-frugal heuristics; (*ii*) introduces their basics, outlining conditions under which fast-and-frugal heuristics perform well or not; (*iii*) challenges established beliefs and clears common misconceptions related to fast-and-frugal heuristics; (*iv*) outlines principles of smart (heuristics-based) management; and (*v*) surveys open questions to explore how the potential of fast-and-frugal heuristics for management mathematics can be fully realized.
konstantinos_katsikopoulos.bio: Back in the day, Konstantinos studied applied mathematics, operations research, computer science, psychology, cognitive science and industrial engineering in Greece, Germany and the USA. After working at the ABC Group of the Max Planck Institute for no less than 13 years, and at MIT for 2 years that felt much longer, he managed to shed (most) disciplinary biases and dedicate himself to researching any interesting facet of human and artificial decision-making. He is currently a professor at the University of Southampton Business School, UK, where he attempts to find proper roles of fast-and-frugal heuristics for operational decisions in the wild.

ozgur_simsek.title: Classification in the Wild
ozgur_simsek.abstract: The desire to assign a person to a class — friend or foe, trustworthy or not — is inherent in human nature. It provides bricks from which intelligence is built. Without forming classes, we would notice only the particular, and any general idea would be out of reach. But how do we make classifications? And how should we? In exploring these questions, we will leave the controlled conditions of the laboratory experiment and enter the fundamental uncertainty of the wild, where the future is not knowable and uncertainty cannot be meaningfully reduced to probability. In this workshop, we will explore precise, formal models of classification — tallying and fast-and-frugal trees — and learn how to construct them. These models are derived from the human abilities of counting and ordering. They give us tools to make fast, accurate, and transparent decisions in real-world situations. They are easy to understand, memorize, teach, and execute. They are descriptive, describing what experienced practitioners actually do, and also prescriptive, suggesting how their practices can be improved.
ozgur_simsek.bio: Özgür Şimşek is a Professor of Artificial Intelligence and the director of the Centre for Artificial Intelligence at the University of Bath. Her research focuses on machine learning, drawing from a multi-disciplinary background that encompasses computer science, engineering, operational research, and psychology. Prof Şimşek received her PhD in Computer Science in 2008 from the University of Massachusetts Amherst. Her research spans a broad range of areas in machine learning and artificial intelligence, including reinforcement learning, supervised learning, learning from small data sets, and bounded rationality. Prof Şimşek is a Co-Investigator, and Machine Learning Theme Lead, of UKRI Centre for Doctoral Training in Accountable, Responsible and Transparent Artificial Intelligence (ART-AI).

peter_todd.title: FORAGING IN MIND, WITH HEURISTICS AND AI COLLABORATORS
peter_todd.abstract: How do we decide when to search for something better and when to stick with what we’ve got? People, like other organisms, must adaptively trade off between exploring and exploiting their environment to obtain the resources they need. This applies to whatever space they are searching, ranging from the external physical world, looking for objects or people or patches of food, to the internal information realm, looking for concepts in memory or knowledge online. Common underlying mechanisms may be used to address the explore/exploit tradeoff in each of these domains: People use similar heuristic strategies to decide when to keep looking and when to give up searching for patchy resources in space (e.g., for fish in a pond) and in mind (e.g., for words in a category). Moreover, the connections between search in these domains may have deep evolutionary roots, leading us to behave similarly in physical and digital environments. At the same time, the structure of the digital environment is changing rapidly through advances in AI, which can make heuristics that were previously ecologically rational become ineffective. But while AI can make some of the search tasks that humans have engaged in no longer necessary, there are ways that AI can enhance human search abilities rather than replace them, as I will point to in this talk.
peter_todd.bio: Peter M. Todd grew up in Silicon Valley, received a PhD in psychology from Stanford University, and in 1995 moved to Germany to help found the Center for Adaptive behaviour and Cognition (ABC), based at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin. The Center's work on decision-making was captured in the books Simple Heuristics That Make Us Smart (OUP, 1999\) and Ecological Rationality: Intelligence in the World (OUP, 2012). Todd moved to Indiana University in 2005 where his research focuses on the cognitive mechanisms that people use to search for and make decisions about adaptively important resources—including mates, information, and food—in space and time, both present and future. His work on search led to the book Cognitive Search: Evolution, Algorithms, and the Brain (Todd, Hills, and Robbins, eds.; MIT Press, 2012). He has served as the Director of the IU Cognitive Science Program and the first Director of the IU Food Institute.

tomas_lejarraga.title: How experimental methods shaped views on human competence and rationality.
tomas_lejarraga.abstract: Within just 7 years, behavioural decision research in psychology underwent a dramatic change: In 1967, Peterson and Beach (1967) reviewed more than 160 experiments concerned with people’s statistical intuitions. Invoking the metaphor of the mind as an intuitive statistician, they concluded that “probability theory and statistics can be used as the basis for psychological models that integrate and account for human performance in a wide range of inferential tasks” (p. 29). Yet in a 1974 Science article, Tversky and Kahneman rejected this conclusion, arguing that “people rely on a limited number of heuristic principles which reduce the complex tasks of assessing probabilities and predicting values to simple judgemental operations” (p. 1124). With that, they introduced the heuristics-and-biases research program, which has profoundly altered how psychology, and the behavioural sciences more generally, view the mind’s competences and rationality. How was this radical transformation possible? We examine a previously neglected driver: The heuristics-and-biases program established an experimental protocol in behavioural decision research that relied on described scenarios rather than learning and experience.
tomas_lejarraga.bio: Tomás is the director of the Decision Science Group at the Universitat de les Illes Balears in Spain and an associate researcher at the Center for Adaptive Rationality (ARC) in the Max Planck Institute for Human Development. He received his PhD from the Universitat Pompeu Fabra and was a postdoctoral fellow at the Carnegie Mellon University. He also spent four years as a research scientist at ARC in Berlin. He is interested in how individuals and groups explore, process, and integrate symbolic information and personally experience information when making decisions under risk and uncertainty.

shahar_hechtlinger.title: How people make life’s most important decisions
shahar_hechtlinger.abstract: Judgement and decision-making research typically studies simplified, low-stakes choices among Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic (WEIRD) samples, rather than real-life decisions that can transform lives—such as emigrating, getting divorced, or changing careers—made by people across the globe. This workshop will explore recent work that lays the conceptual and empirical foundation for understanding the psychology of these transformative life decisions, and give participants the opportunity to engage interactively with the topic. The first part will present a conceptual framework of transformative life decisions. Drawing on principles of bounded and ecological rationality, this framework identifies key dimensions of such decisions and specifies simple, largely non-compensatory choice strategies that address these dimensions. It will also cover the challenge that transformative life decisions pose to predicting the behavioural impact of new technological advances (e.g., AI companions). Building on this framework, the second part will present empirical work from five studies with over 4,000 participants spanning the UK, the US, India, Nigeria, and Germany. Using a mixed-methods approach combining manual text classification, large language models, and quantitative surveys, this work shows that transformative life decisions are common experiences across cultures and that people most commonly rely on simple strategies associated with subjective outcomes as favorable as complex deliberation. Yet in WEIRD countries, most individuals recommend complex, maximising strategies to others, revealing an “ideal–reality gap” that is absent elsewhere. These findings suggest that maximising is a culturally specific belief rather than universal behaviour, challenging a long tradition of applying laboratory-derived standards to real-world decision-making.
shahar_hechtlinger.bio: Shahar Hechtlinger is a postdoctoral fellow at the Center for Adaptive Rationality (ARC) at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development. She studies search and choice processes in the real world, particularly under conditions of radical uncertainty and limited experience-based knowledge. Her current research explores how people make some of the most important decisions in their lives—such as emigrating or fleeing a country, ending a relationship, or engaging in acts of ‘civic heroism’ like disclosing sexual assault by a person in power. Her work is informed by a long-standing interest in philosophy of science and the meta-scientific questions of how the behavioural sciences study—and should study—consequential real-world phenomena. She recently received the prestigious de Finetti Award for her work on transformative life decisions.

kamil_fulawka.title: Large language models accurately identify decision reasons in verbal reports
kamil_fulawka.abstract: Understanding the reasons behind human choices under risk is a central goal of decision scientists, but traditional methods relying on behavioural data are limited by strict invariance assumptions. We introduce a scalable analytical framework using large language models (LLMs) to analyze verbal reports and identify articulated reasons for choice between monetary lotteries. A validated LLM accurately identified predefined decision reasons in participants' free-text reports, aligning with their actual choices in 95% of trials. Our analysis reveals that the reasons behind people's decisions vary systematically and are driven more by the structure of the choice problem than by individual differences. Crucially, reasons identified from verbal reports yield more parsimonious and informative representations of decision processes compared to those inferred from choices alone; furthermore, problem-specific reason profiles achieve out-of-sample prediction accuracy that is competitive with established computational models. This work demonstrates that verbal reports are a rich data source and our analytical framework can unlock their potential, delivering results that challenge the field's foundational invariance assumptions and pave the way for more context-sensitive and interpretable models of human decision-making.
kamil_fulawka.bio: I am a computational psychologist and researcher at the Center Synergy of Systems at TU Dresden. My work combines behavioural experiments and computational approaches, including large language models, to study decision-making. I focus on how emotions, heuristics, and digital environments shape behaviour in domains such as digital health and politics.

levin_brinkmann.title: When Machines Expand Human Problem-Solving
levin_brinkmann.abstract:
Many heuristics shaping our cognition are acquired culturally rather than discovered individually. Through social learning, useful strategies and behavioural traits are transmitted and preserved across generations. Today's AI systems are products of this same process. But the relationship is no longer one-directional. Machines are now feeding back into human culture in multifaceted ways. I will focus this talk on two prototypical effects.

The first is adaptive adoption. We reproduce the "AlphaGo effect" in a lab-based experiment and show that machine-discovered strategies can persistently shift human problem-solving across generations. In particular, we show that these strategies propagate through human populations when they are non-trivial, learnable, and offer a recognizable advantage, and thereby expand the frontier of human problem-solving.

The second is behavioural drift. I will present work on the "delve effect," showing that passive exposure to AI chatbots at scale measurably shifts human language, without requiring deliberate intent.

Together, these findings shows that we are entering an age of machine culture — where AI is no longer merely a tool we use, but a force shaping our behaviour.
levin_brinkmann.bio: Levin Brinkmann is a research scientist at the Center for Humans and Machines, Max Planck Institute for Human Development. He studies how humans and AI shape each other's behaviour and culture. His work spans cultural evolution in human-AI systems, emergent norms in LLM populations, and AI-driven discovery at the edges of human culture.

krishna_gummadi.title: Engineering the Psychology of Machine Judgment Under Uncertainty
krishna_gummadi.abstract: At their core, emerging agentic AI architectures enable AI models, such as Large Language Models (LLMs), to extend their functionality by calling external tools (e.g., web search). When solving challenging tasks, LLMs can strategically augment their internal (parametric) knowledge, skills, and abilities by utilizing external tools. In the process, LLMs need to make multiple crucial decisions ranging from whether to call (or not to call) a tool to whether much to trust (or not trust) the responses provided by the tools. In this talk, I will discuss our recent and ongoing attempts to develop principled frameworks to assess and enhance LLM judgments, when making decisions related to using tools. Our frameworks are inspired by existing theories on human decision making and human judgment under uncertainty.
krishna_gummadi.bio: Krishna Gummadi is a Scientific Director at the Max Planck Institute for Software Systems (MPI-SWS) and a Professor of Computer Science at University of Saarland in Germany.  He received his Ph.D. (2005) and B.Tech. (2000) degrees in Computer Science and Engineering from the University of Washington and the Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, respectively. His recent research focuses on understanding and building social computing systems that are fair, accountable, explainable, and transparent. Krishna's work on fair and transparent machine learning, online social networks and media platforms, Internet access networks, and peer-to-peer systems has been widely cited and his papers have received numerous (15 best/outstanding paper) awards, including Test of Time Awards at ACM SIGCOMM and AAAI ICWSM. He also received an ERC advanced grant on "Foundations for Fair Social Computing".

rui_mata.title: Advancing Cognitive Science with Language Models
rui_mata.abstract: Cognitive science faces longstanding challenges in research integration, formalization, and other areas, in part due to its multifaceted and interdisciplinary nature. Recent advances in artificial intelligence, particularly the development of large language models, offer tools that may help to address these issues. We outline how language models can support areas where the field has historically struggled, including establishing cross-disciplinary connections, formalizing theories, developing clear measurement taxonomies, achieving generalizability through integrated modelling frameworks, and capturing contextual and individual variation. Taken together, we conclude that language models could serve as tools for a more integrative and cumulative cognitive science when used judiciously to complement, rather than replace, human agency.
rui_mata.bio: Rui Mata studied psychology at the University of Lisbon and completed his PhD at the Freie Universität Berlin. He has held research positions across Europe and the United States, including at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development. Since 2014, he is a Professor for Cognitive and Decision Sciences at the University of Basel. His research examines how people perceive and make decisions about risk across the lifespan, focusing on the cognitive processes that shape choices affecting health, wealth, and sustainability.

dirk_wulff.abstract: Psychology has a conceptual clarity problem: thousands of constructs and measures with unclear boundaries, overlapping definitions, and inconsistent terminology, often referred to as jingle-jangle fallacies. Sorting out these relationships empirically would require prohibitively costly research designs. In this talk, I show how large language models can help map the relationships between psychological constructs, measures, and observable behaviour at a scale that was previously impractical. I first present work showing that semantic embeddings from language models can predict empirical associations between survey items and scales, automatically detect jingle-jangle fallacies, and suggest more parsimonious taxonomies. I then present ongoing work that extends this approach beyond survey-based measures to behavioural tasks using task embeddings, vector representations that capture not only what a task asks but how people perform it. Together, these lines of work validate a generative approach that can help bring clarity and map out psychology's nomological net.
dirk_wulff.bio: Dirk U. Wulff is a senior research scientist at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin, where he works in the Center for Adaptive Rationality and leads the 'Learning' Research Area. A psychologist by training, he bridges cognitive science and artificial intelligence, using computational methods to understand learning, mental representations, and decision-making in both humans and artificial agents.

raluca_rilla.title: Detecting and Preventing LLM Pollution in Online Studies
raluca_rilla.abstract: A substantial and growing share of online study responses reflects the influence of Large Language Models (LLMs). LLM-assisted responses pose a more serious threat because their convincing output can reshape response distributions in harmful ways. We term this phenomenon "LLM Pollution," composed of three parts: Partial LLM Mediation, Full LLM Delegation, and LLM Spillover, capturing the spectrum from participants using models to polish their answers, to agentic systems completing studies autonomously, to behavioural shifts among participants who suspect AI involvement. Because these forms vary in technical sophistication and human oversight, no single detection measure is sufficient. We will begin the workshop with a more detailed look into the LLM Pollution framework. Participants will then experience the capabilities of current agentic systems by deploying a simple browser-based agent to complete an online study (no prior experience with agents is needed). From there, participants will (i) develop in-survey measures for detecting LLM agents and LLM-assisted responses, and (ii) reflect on broader steps the research community must take to address this growing threat. We will conclude by synthesizing insights across groups and discussing the path forward.
raluca_rilla.bio: Raluca Rilla is a doctoral student at the Center for Humans and Machines at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development through the International Max Planck Research School on Learning, Institutions, and Future Evolution, supervised by Prof. Dr. Iyad Rahwan. She holds an MSc in Cognitive Systems from the University of Potsdam and a BA in Cognitive Science and Psychology from Franklin & Marshall College. Her research examines how human behaviour evolves as AI systems become embedded in decision-making environments, with a particular interest in how sustained interaction with these technologies can reshape social and ethical norms. This focus extends to the integrity of the research process, where Raluca investigates how both the autonomous participation of agentic AI and human use of LLMs in surveys can result in misleading scientific conclusions.

jason_burton.title: How large language models can reshape collective intelligence
jason_burton.abstract: Collective intelligence underpins the success of groups, organisations, markets, and societies. Through distributed cognition and coordination, collectives can achieve outcomes that exceed the capabilities of individuals—even experts—resulting in improved accuracy and novel capabilities. Often, collective intelligence is supported by information technology, such as online prediction markets that elicit the ‘wisdom of crowds’, online forums that structure collective deliberation, or digital platforms that crowdsource knowledge from the public. Large language models, however, are transforming how information is aggregated, accessed, and transmitted online. Here we focus on the unique opportunities and challenges this transformation poses for collective intelligence. We bring together interdisciplinary perspectives from industry and academia to identify potential benefits, risks, policy-relevant considerations, and open research questions, culminating in a call for a closer examination of how large language models affect humans’ ability to collectively tackle complex problems.
jason_burton.bio: Jason is a tenure-track assistant professor of psychology and social data science at the University of Copenhagen and an associate research scientist at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development. He researches how digital technologies, like recommender systems and large language models, affect the way people think and behave. His work has been published in journals such as *Nature Human Behaviour* and *Cognition* and has received special recognition from the Knight First Amendment Institute, ACM Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI), International Conference on Computational Social Science, and ACM Collective Intelligence, among others.

nikolas_zoeller.title: Improving Medical Diagnosis with Human-AI Collectives
nikolas_zoeller.abstract: This talk examines the potential of hybrid human-AI collectives to improve performance in high-stakes decision problems such as open-ended medical diagnosis. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have generated considerable interest in their use for clinical decision support, yet substantial concerns remain regarding reliability, bias, hallucinations, and the risks of relying on AI systems in isolation. Drawing primarily on work combining more than 40,000 physician-generated differential diagnoses, five LLMs, and over 2,100 clinical case vignettes, I will show that hybrid collectives of physicians and AI systems can outperform individual clinicians, standalone models, and collectives composed solely of humans or solely of LLMs. I will then discuss the methodological foundations of these results, including the harmonization of open-ended diagnostic responses, aggregation procedures that account for rank and source quality, and the role of metadata and domain knowledge in improving collective solutions. Building on this foundation, I will present current work on social feedback in hybrid collective problem solving. Two recent experiments extend the aggregation perspective by examining how advice and deliberation from human, AI, and hybrid sources shape diagnostic reasoning and revision. Across these studies, I explore both the opportunities and the limitations of combining human and AI intelligence in medical diagnosis.
nikolas_zoeller.bio: Nikolas Zöller is a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Southern Denmark (SDU) in Odense and a guest researcher at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development (MPIB) in Berlin. His research focuses on hybrid collective intelligence, with a particular interest in how artificial intelligence can help individuals and groups make better decisions in domains such as medical diagnostics. Before joining SDU, he was a postdoctoral researcher at MPIB, a research associate at the Institute for Urban Futures at Potsdam University of Applied Sciences, and worked as a freelance data scientist and software developer. He holds a PhD in Data Engineering from Constructor University Bremen and an MSc in Physics from Freie Universität Berlin.

stefan_herzog_julian_berger.title: Learning in human-AI interaction: Boosting vs. handicapping human competences
stefan_herzog_julian_berger.abstract:
Much attention in research and public discourse focuses on artificial intelligence and machine learning, while human learning remains an understudied aspect of human-AI interaction. In a recent meta-analysis (Berger et al., 2026), we found that only a minority of human-AI interaction studies allowed participants to learn from outcome feedback---meaning most participants could neither track their own or the AI's performance nor learn when and how to adaptively rely on AI. It is thus in our view unsurprising that, on average, humans with AI advice underperform relative to the AI alone. In contrast, our meta-analysis shows that humans tend to outperform AI when study designs allow for learning.

In this workshop, we present our perspective on the unsatisfying state of the human-AI interaction literature and the paradigm shift needed to achieve the promise of human-AI synergy (i.e., humans with AI achieving superior outcomes compared to either acting alone). We argue for a research approach that fosters rather than handicaps human learning, and that can reveal the mechanisms that foster human-AI synergy. We present recent evidence for learning as a key driver of human-AI synergy (Berger et al., 2026\) and, through a series of short interactive exercises, demonstrate the boundary conditions such as task predictability, feedback clarity, and delay that shape the effectiveness of offering learning opportunities. The workshop aims to advance a perspective on human-AI interaction design that prioritizes boosting human competences (Herzog & Franklin, 2024).
stefan_herzog_julian_berger.bio:
Stefan Herzog ([stefanherzog.org](http://stefanherzog.org)) is head of the “Boosting Decision Making” research area ([scienceofboosting.org](http://scienceofboosting.org)) and senior researcher at the Center for Adaptive Rationality at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin. He studies how to improve human judgment and decision making by investigating human and machine behavior. His work combines insights and methods from cognitive science, heuristics, AI, hybrid collective intelligence, and behavioral public policy. He also works on applications in online environments and in hybrid human–AI medical diagnostics. His research has been published in leading journals, featured in Science and Scientific American, and covered in books like Superforecasting. He received the Hillel Einhorn New Investigator Award in 2010 and holds a doctorate in psychology from the University of Basel.

Julian is a postdoctoral researcher in the Strategic Organization Design group at the University of Southern Denmark and starts as an Assistant Professor of Management at the Universitat Pompeu Fabra in September. He studies the combination of human and artificial decision making capabilities, ranging from simple models built with tabular data to more complex models for processing images and language. Psychologist by day, computer scientist by night, Julian is looking forward to the day he can offload his tax declaration to AI but thinks we should remain cognitively independent and vigilant of deskilling ourselves in the meantime.

zoe_rahwan.title: How delegating decisions to AI can shape dishonesty
zoe_rahwan.abstract:
We know that delegating tasks to AI can drive impressive productivity gains. This is set to accelerate with increased usage of agentic AI systems, such as Open Claw. Yet, giving tasks to AI agents to complete autonomously also raises ethical risks. Are we inadvertently building systems that make it easier to delegate unethical behaviour?

In this talk, I will share recent experimental work examining what happens when human principals use AI agents to perform common experimental tasks with clear incentives to cheat. Our findings reveal a troubling pattern: people are significantly more likely to request cheating when they don't have to explicitly ask for it, such as when they can induce cheating through high-level goal setting or supervised learning.

Amid the emergence of large language models (LLMs), we also examined how instructing AI agents with natural language affected honesty, relative to human agents. While participants did not always ask AI agents to cheat more than human agents, there were stark results when it came to compliance with instructions. AI agents largely complied with fully unethical instructions while humans most commonly resisted. Given the apparent failure of inbuilt guardrails to inhibit cheating across popular LLMs, we explored bespoke guardrail strategies. With such strategies, compliance with unethical instructions could be curbed, but usually not eliminated. We will discuss design and policy strategies to mitigate ethical risks of delegating to AI.
zoe_rahwan.bio: Zoe Rahwan is a Research Scientist at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin. Her behavioural science research is centred on moral decision-making, and in particular the drivers of dishonesty and charitable giving. With over 15 years of public policy experience in central banking and financial regulation, she undertakes research with direct policy applications, commonly working with practitioners, such as bankers, regulators and professional fundraisers. Her aim is to better understand the factors that influence ethical behaviour, in support of policy-making. She generates behavioural insights for real-world challenges through field experiments, online experiments and large-scale games, publishing in leading journals, including *Nature*.

anastasia_kozyreva.title: Free Speech vs. Harmful Misinformation: The Psychology and Politics of Online Speech Governance
anastasia_kozyreva.abstract: Social media platforms act as de facto regulators of online speech, yet their governance decisions are increasingly shaped by political pressure rather than evidence—most visibly in the widening transatlantic divergence between EU regulatory tightening and US platform deregulation. At the heart of this divergence lies a genuine conflict between two competing values: freedom of expression as a near-absolute liberty and the protection of the epistemic conditions under which meaningful expression is possible. This talk examines that conflict from three angles: the political and normative landscape structuring current governance debates; the psychology of people’s attitudes towards free speech, misinformation, and harm; and empirical evidence on public attitudes to content moderation and misinformation interventions. I present findings from a large pre-registered cross-national survey with conjoint components across six European countries and the US (N \= 10,663; 2024–25), examining how citizens trade off the two values and how their choices are shaped by both the structure of the problem and individual characteristics. The talk concludes by using these findings as a starting point for an evidence-informed discussion of the political tensions and normative stakes behind the free speech versus harm prevention debate—and of what it would take to govern online speech in ways that are both effective and publicly legitimate.
anastasia_kozyreva.bio: Anastasia Kozyreva is a senior research scientist at the Center for Adaptive Rationality (ARC) at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin, where she leads the research area "Cognition in online environments". With a background in philosophy, her work bridges disciplines, combining conceptual and empirical approaches to understand the cognitive and ethical implications of digital technologies for society. Her current research examines how citizens navigate trade-offs between freedom of expression and protection from online misinformation, media literacy interventions against misinformation, and the drivers of democratic alienation.

mario_scharfbillig.title: Fractured reality – How democracy can win the global struggle over the information space
mario_scharfbillig.abstract: The report examines the evolving impact of digital technologies on European democracy. In an era of a global struggle to control the information space, this report offers critical insights into the fracturing of perceived realities, the rise of the ”fantasy-industrial complex,” and the systemic risks posed by the attention economy. It exposes how platforms algorithmically prioritise engagement over accuracy, reinforcing ideological echo chambers and amplifying mis- and disin­ formation that erode democratic resilience. The report highlights how structural incentives and foreign control of the most important players in the information space undermine information integrity, collective knowledge and civic discourse. In response, the report presents a number of recommendations, from fostering alternative public spaces and crowd-sourced knowledge systems to reforming business models, restoring user agency, and advancing EU digital sovereignty through decentralised infrastructure.
mario_scharfbillig.bio: Mario Scharfbillig uses behavioural insights to improve evidence-informed policymaking and democratic processes in the EU. He works at the Joint Research Centre of the European Commission, leading the [Enlightenment 2.0 research programme](https://knowledge4policy.ec.europa.eu/evidence-informed-policy-making/topic/enlightenment-20_en). The programme seeks to provide the interdisciplinary scientific underpinning to improve the evolution of the political decision-making process in an increasingly polarised, complex and uncertain present. He is also a member of several advisery groups, including for Horizon projects, ALLEA, Club of Venice, EGU and ISC. He received his PhD in economics from the University of Mainz, where he specialised in research on behavioural economics, public policy and corporate governance. For his [work on advising](https://knowledge4policy.ec.europa.eu/projects-activities/values-identities-policymakers-guide_en) policymakers, he was named among the "50+1 Influential Researchers Whose Work Could Help Shape 21st Century Politicians" by Apolitical.

renee_diresta.title: Invisible Rulers, Visible Costs: Propaganda, AI, and the Fight for Shared Reality
renee_diresta.abstract: The information environment shaping contemporary belief formation is not a neutral medium but an engineered system. Recommender algorithms, influencer economies, and now generative AI have collectively altered the scale, speed, and character of rumor, producing bespoke realities: personalized epistemic worlds in which communities disagree not only about values but about facts. These are environments engineered to exploit the shortcuts people reasonably use to navigate information — manufacturing familiarity, simulating consensus, rendering identity suspect. Evidence-like material — images, audio, video, text, personas — has become cheap to manufacture, while the things that make evidence credible — trust, attention, verification, institutional legitimacy — have become expensive. The result is a discernment tax levied on attention: a burden that used to be absorbed by editorial processes, expert institutions, and the sheer friction of production, now shifted onto individual receivers who must adjudicate authenticity in real time, at scale. This lecture traces how propaganda, persuasion, and public discourse have been reshaped by platform architectures and generative AI, and what this means for the prospects of a shared factual basis for democratic life. It also confronts a harder question: what happens when the study of these dynamics itself becomes a target? Drawing on first-hand experience with congressional investigations, lawsuits, and coordinated harassment campaigns directed at researchers in this field — including the dismantling of the Stanford Internet Observatory — I will reflect on the institutional, legal, and psychological pressures that now bear on empirical work in contested domains. Defending scientific integrity has never been a solitary act; in an automated and adversarial information ecosystem, it is increasingly a collective one.
renee_diresta.bio: Renée DiResta is a social media researcher and the author of *Invisible Rulers: The People Who Turn Lies into Reality*. As an Associate Research Professor at Georgetown University's McCourt School of Public Policy, she studies how information systems are manipulated, and how they can be reimagined — with a particular focus on middleware, decentralized governance, and increasing user agency in digital environments.

gerd_gigerenzer.title: On AI-Pollution: Will AI contaminate science?
gerd_gigerenzer.abstract:
AI is celebrated as a disruptive technology. It is now beginning to disrupt science itself — and not for the better. I draw on my experience as Vice-President of the European Research Council (ERC) and on my research on paper mills. The ERC permits researchers to use large language models (LLMs) to write proposals, but forbids reviewers from using them to write evaluations. The second rule is increasingly ignored; the first has brought the ERC close to collapse. In 2025, submissions to ERC calls rose by an unprecedented 30–40% over 2024, largely driven by the use of LLMs. This mass production of proposals creates an intolerable burden on reviewers, and a predictable decline in the quality of peer review and funding decisions. Similar surges are being reported by other funding agencies and in AI conference submissions.

The problem does not stop there. Paper mills now use LLMs to manufacture articles with fraudulent data at an industrial scale. Researchers under career pressure pay paper mills five-digit sums to be listed as authors on these fake papers in order to improve their metrics. Paper mills, in turn, bribe journal editors with six-digit sums to place these papers in special issues. Gold Open Access has unwittingly helped create this market, much as it has fueled predatory journals and enriched large publishing companies whose business model rewards volume over quality. And every academic who evaluates applicants by counting publications rather than judging their substance becomes, knowingly or not, an accomplice in this contamination of science by commerce. At a time when science is already under political and social attack, this contamination risks destroying something far more valuable than research budgets: public trust in science itself.
gerd_gigerenzer.bio: Gerd Gigerenzer, long-time director at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development, is former Professor of Psychology at the University of Chicago and member of the Berlin-Brandenburg Academy of Sciences, the German Academy of Sciences, the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, and the American Philosophical Society. He was awarded honorary doctorates from the University of Basel, the Open University of the Netherlands, and the University of Southampton. Awards include the AAAS Prize for the best article in the behavioural sciences, the German Psychology Award, and the Communicator Award of the German Research Foundation. His award-winning popular books *Calculated Risks*, *Gut Feelings, Risk Savvy, and How To Stay Smart In A Smart World*  have been translated into more than 20 languages. He has trained U.S. federal judges, physicians, and top managers in decision-making. The Swiss Duttweiler Institute has distinguished Gigerenzer as one of the top-100 Global Thought Leaders worldwide.
